title: The body is closer to the truth than the inner monologue.
body: Your mind will give you a story. Your body will give you data. Both matter, but in any disagreement, the body's data is usually closer to what's actually happening.
reflectionQuestion: What does the inner monologue say you're doing today — and what does your body say?

title: Survey before you try to fix.
body: You can't regulate what you haven't located. Five minutes of noticing — feet to head — beats fifty minutes of advice from your phone about how to feel better.
reflectionQuestion: What part of your body have you been treating as background noise?

title: Name the state. The intervention follows.
body: Regulated needs nothing. Activated needs somewhere for the energy to go. Shut-down needs the smallest possible movement. Most advice fails because it's matched to the wrong state.
reflectionQuestion: Right now: regulated, activated, or shut-down?

title: Exhale longer than you inhale.
body: It's the single most reliable regulation tool you have. Free, portable, mechanical. Practice it in calm so it's there when stress arrives.
reflectionQuestion: When today would three rounds of 4-in, 6-out have been useful?

title: Tension is information you haven't listened to yet.
body: Tight shoulders, clenched jaw, knotted stomach — the body is reporting. Asking 'what are you about?' is more useful than asking 'how do I make this go away?'
reflectionQuestion: What has your tightest spot been carrying this week?

title: Run HALT before you trust the mood.
body: Half the moods that wreck a day come from hunger, anger, loneliness, or tiredness. The check is two seconds. Eat first. Decide later.
reflectionQuestion: When did you last let HALT win a decision you'd have made differently after lunch?

title: Sleep is not a virtue. It's a biological floor.
body: Six-hour sleep, sustained, equals legally impaired cognitive performance. Fixing the wake time + getting morning light is the highest-leverage move you can make this week.
reflectionQuestion: If you had to pick one sleep change, which would actually stick?

title: Move first. Mood follows.
body: Twenty minutes of walking, three times a week — the mood effect is comparable to first-line antidepressants for mild-moderate cases. Don't wait until you feel like it.
reflectionQuestion: Where in your week is there ten minutes you could give to a walk?

title: Borrow a nervous system you trust.
body: You're not designed to regulate alone. Co-regulation isn't weakness — it's the architecture. Identify your calm anchor and be near them more often than you currently are.
reflectionQuestion: Who's your calm anchor — and when did you last sit with them with no agenda?

title: Three questions. Once a day. Like a friend.
body: Where am I in my body? What state am I in? What does my body need? Treat the answers the way you'd treat a friend who told you the same. Sixty seconds. Two weeks of it and your baseline shifts.
reflectionQuestion: When in your day could the 60-second check-in actually fit?

title: Your body is not a vehicle.
body: Treating the body as transportation for the head is the default and it's expensive. The body has its own intelligence. Listening to it is not woo; it's the most basic act of self-knowledge.
reflectionQuestion: What would treating your body as part of you change about tomorrow?

title: Kindness is the method, not the reward.
body: Self-criticism predicts worse behaviour change. Self-kindness predicts better. The kinder internal stance — the one you'd take with a friend — is the more effective stance.
reflectionQuestion: What would you say to a friend whose body was as tired as yours is today?
